content.text: According to Hale's **teenager stage framework**, some degree of withdrawal is developmentally typical — teens are actively building autonomy and an inner life that feels separate from family, which is healthy — but the most useful thing you can do right now is **stay available without pressing**: keep low-stakes, side-by-side moments in your week (a car ride, cooking together, watching a show they choose) because teens tend to open up when nothing is formally "on the table." That works because it removes the pressure of a face-to-face check-in, which can feel interrogative to a teenager who's already pulling inward. As an optional next step, the framework does flag a few things worth watching: noticeable shifts in sleep, appetite, or school performance — if you're seeing any of those alongside the withdrawal, it's worth a conversation with your pediatric or family practice office, just to rule out anything that needs support beyond what a parent can provide at home.
